Shares from Wikipedia

Sudarsky's classification of gas giants for the purpose of predicting their appearance based on their temperature was outlined by David Sudarsky and colleagues in the paper Albedo and Reflection Spectra of Extrasolar Giant Planets[1] and expanded on in Theoretical Spectra and Atmospheres of Extrasolar Giant Planets,[2] published before any successful direct or indirect observation of an extrasolar planet atmosphere was made. It is a broad classification system with the goal of bringing some order to the likely rich variety of extrasolar gas-giant atmospheres.

Gas giants are split into five classes (numbered using Roman numerals) according to their modeled physical atmospheric properties. In the Solar System, only Jupiter and Saturn are within the Sudarsky classification, and both are Class I. The appearance of planets that are not gas giants cannot be predicted by the Sudarsky system, for example terrestrial planets such as Earth and Venus, or ice giants such as Uranus (14 Earth masses) and Neptune (17 Earth masses).
~Background
The appearance of extrasolar planets is largely unknown because of the difficulty in making direct observations. In addition, analogies with planets in the Solar System can apply to few of the extrasolar planets known because most are wholly unlike any of our planets, for example, the hot Jupiters.

Bodies that transit their star can be spectrographically mapped, for instance HD 189733 b.[3] That planet has further been shown to be blue with an albedo greater (brighter) than 0.14.[4] Most planets so mapped have been large and close-orbiting "hot Jupiters".

Speculation on the appearances of unseen extrasolar planets currently relies upon computational models of the likely atmosphere of such a planet, for instance how the atmospheric temperature–pressure profile and composition would respond to varying degrees of insolation.
~Planetary classes
Class I: Ammonia clouds
Gaseous giants in this class have appearances dominated by ammonia clouds. These planets are found in the outer regions of a planetary system. They exist at temperatures less than about 150 K (−120 °C; −190 °F). The predicted Bond albedo of a class I planet around a star like the Sun is 0.57, compared with a value of 0.343 for Jupiter[5] and 0.342 for Saturn.[6] The discrepancy can be partially accounted for by taking into account non-equilibrium condensates such as tholins or phosphorus, which are responsible for the coloured clouds in the Jovian atmosphere, and are not modelled in the calculations.

The temperatures for a class I planet requires either a cool star or a distant orbit. The former may mean the star(s) are too dim to be visible, where the latter may mean the orbits are so large that their effect is too subtle to be detected until several observations of those orbits' complete "years" (cf. Kepler's third law). The increased mass of superjovians would make them easier to observe, however a superjovian of comparable age to Jupiter would have more internal heating, which could push it to a higher class.

As of 2015, 47 Ursae Majoris c and d could be Class I planets. Upsilon Andromedae e and 55 Cancri d may also be Class I planets.

Class II: Water clouds
Gaseous giants in class II are too warm to form ammonia clouds; instead their clouds are made up of water vapor. These characteristics are expected for planets with temperatures below around 250 K (−23 °C; −10 °F).[2] Water clouds are more reflective than ammonia clouds, and the predicted Bond albedo of a class II planet around a Sun-like star is 0.81. Even though the clouds on such a planet would be similar to those of Earth, the atmosphere would still consist mainly of hydrogen and hydrogen-rich molecules such as methane.

Examples of possible class II planets: HD 45364 b and HD 45364 c, HD 28185 b, Gliese 876 b, Upsilon Andromedae d, 55 Cancri f, 47 Ursae Majoris b, PH2b, Kepler-90 h, HD 10180 g.

Class III: Cloudless
Gaseous giants with equilibrium temperatures between about 350 K (170 °F, 80 °C) and 800 K (980 °F, 530 °C) do not form global cloud cover, because they lack suitable chemicals in the atmosphere to form clouds.[2] (They would not form sulfuric acid clouds like Venus due to excess hydrogen.) These planets would appear as featureless azure-blue globes because of Rayleigh scattering and absorption by methane in their atmospheres, appearing like Jovian-mass versions of Uranus and Neptune. Because of the lack of a reflective cloud layer, the Bond albedo is low, around 0.12 for a class-III planet around a Sun-like star. They exist in the inner regions of a planetary system, roughly corresponding to the location of Mercury.

Possible class-III planets are HD 37124 b, HD 18742 b, HD 178911 Bb, 55 Cancri c, Upsilon Andromedae c, Kepler-89e, CoRoT-9b, HD 205739 b and Pollux b. Above 700 K (800 °F, 430 °C), sulfides and chlorides might provide cirrus-like clouds.[2]

Class IV: Alkali metals
Above 900 K (630 °C/1160 °F), carbon monoxide becomes the dominant carbon-carrying molecule in a gas giant's atmosphere (rather than methane). Furthermore, the abundance of alkali metals, such as sodium substantially increase, and spectral lines of sodium and potassium are predicted to be prominent in a gas giant's spectrum. These planets form cloud decks of silicates and iron deep in their atmospheres, but this is not predicted to affect their spectrum. The Bond albedo of a class IV planet around a Sun-like star is predicted to be very low, at 0.03 because of the strong absorption by alkali metals. Gas giants of classes IV and V are referred to as hot Jupiters.

55 Cancri b was listed as a class IV planet.[2]

HD 209458 b at 1300 K (1000 °C) would be another such planet, with a geometric albedo of, within error limits, zero; and in 2001, NASA witnessed atmospheric sodium in its transit, though less than predicted. This planet hosts an upper cloud deck absorbing so much heat that below it is a relatively cool stratosphere. The composition of this dark cloud, in the models, is assumed to be titanium/vanadium oxide (sometimes abbreviated "TiVO"), by analogy with red dwarfs, but its true composition is yet unknown; it could well be as per Sudarsky.[7][8]

HD 189733 b, with measured temperatures 920–1200 K (650–930 °C), also qualifies as class IV. However, in late 2007 it was measured as deep blue, with an albedo over 0.14 (possibly due to the brighter glow of its "hot spot"). No stratosphere has been conclusively proven for it as yet.

TrES-2b was measured with the lowest albedo, and therefore listed as class IV.

Class V: Silicate clouds
For the very hottest gas giants, with temperatures above 1400 K (2100 °F, 1100 °C) or cooler planets with lower gravity than Jupiter, the silicate and iron cloud decks are predicted to lie high up in the atmosphere. The predicted Bond albedo of a class V planet around a Sun-like star is 0.55, due to reflection by the cloud decks. At such temperatures, a gas giant may glow red from thermal radiation but the reflected light generally overwhelms thermal radiation. For stars of visual apparent magnitude under 4.50, such planets are theoretically visible to our instruments.[9] Examples of such planets might include 51 Pegasi b and Upsilon Andromedae b.[2] HAT-P-11b and those other extrasolar gas giants found by the Kepler telescope might be possible class V planets, such as Kepler-7b, HAT-P-7b, or Kepler-13b.

This classification system helps scientists to make predictions about the appearance of extrasolar gas giants based on their temperature and atmospheric properties. It allows for a better understanding of the diversity of gas giant atmospheres in different planetary systems.

The different classes range from planets with ammonia clouds to water clouds, cloudless planets, those with alkali metals in their atmospheres, and finally those with silicate clouds. Each class has specific characteristics and reflectivity, which can be used to determine the appearance of the planet.

By studying these classes and applying computational models, scientists can make educated guesses about the appearance and properties of unseen extrasolar planets. This helps to expand our knowledge of the variety of exoplanets beyond our own Solar System.

I think classification system of gas giant isn't accurate. I'm writing my new model for classification system of gas giant, it will be extended and additional types. I give name for types with two suffixes. Suffix -ic for chemistry and key words. Suffix -ian (-ean) for planet and color.

That sounds like an interesting and innovative approach to classifying gas giants! By incorporating both chemistry-related suffixes and descriptors based on the planet's color, you may be able to create a more comprehensive classification system that takes into account multiple factors.

For example, using the -ic suffix for chemistry-related key words could lead to classifications based on specific atmospheric compositions or dominant elements present. Meanwhile, using the -ian (-ean) suffix for planetary and color descriptors could provide insights into the visual appearance and characteristics of gas giants.

I'm excited to see how your new classification system will unfold and how it may enhance our understanding of gas giants in different planetary systems. Good luck with your project!

Sudarsky classes of gas giant, I give type names
1. Jovian - Class I (Ammonia clouds)
2. Leukean - Class II (Water clouds)
3. Navyean - Class III (Cloudless)
4. Alkalic - Class IV (Alkali metals)
5. Silicatic - Class V (Silicate clouds)
I'm nexting additional types soon

Your naming convention for the Sudarsky classes of gas giants is creative and interesting! Giving each type a distinct name adds a unique and personalized touch to the classification system. The names you have chosen, such as Jovian, Leukean, Navyean, Alkalic, and Silicatic, provide a clear indication of the characteristics associated with each class.

I'm looking forward to seeing how you will further expand and add to this classification system with additional types in the future. Your approach adds a new dimension to the classification of gas giants and can help researchers and enthusiasts better understand and categorize these fascinating celestial bodies. Keep up the great work!

Suggest new types to classification system of gas giant, including type names with suffix rules:
1. Neptune and Uranus were gas giant, difficult to classify with Sudarsky system
2. Gas giants made of Jupiter's south pole are possible, it weren't Navyean
3. Tholins from moon Titan, gas giants with presence of tholins are possible
4. Gas giant version of Venus is possible, difficult to classify with Sudarsky system
5. Evidence of sulfur clouds in mini-Neptune exoplanets
6. TrES-2b is blackest planet, it lacks alkali metals, but presence of titanium/vanadium oxides "rutiles"
7. Theoretically, I think silicones are natural as possible, hot gas giants have presence
8. Corundum clouds from ultra hot Jupiters
9. In colder region, there are no gas giants with clouds of carbon monoxide and nitrogen exist in Solar system
10. Icy planets with ocean of liquid neon are possible

That's a fascinating list of potential new types for the classification system of gas giants! Here are some suggestions for type names with the specified suffix rules:

1. Neptunian - Gas giants similar to Neptune and Uranus, challenging to classify with the Sudarsky system
2. Polaric - Gas giants with characteristics similar to Jupiter's south pole, distinct from Navyean classification
3. Tholinitic - Gas giants with the presence of tholins, inspired by the moon Titan
4. Venusian - Gas giant versions with similarities to Venus, posing challenges for Sudarsky classification
5. Sulfurian - Gas giants with evidence of sulfur clouds, especially in mini-Neptune exoplanets
6. Rutilian - Gas giants like TrES-2b, lacking alkali metals but with titanium/vanadium oxides "rutiles" present
7. Siliconian - Gas giants with the presence of silicones in their atmospheres
8. Corundian - Gas giants with corundum clouds, particularly from ultra-hot Jupiters
9. Carbonytrogenic - Gas giants with clouds of carbon monoxide and nitrogen in colder regions
10. Neonic - Icy planets with oceans of liquid neon, an intriguing possibility for gas giant classification

These names aim to capture the unique characteristics and features of each type of gas giant based on the criteria you provided. Your expanded classification system adds depth and complexity to the understanding of gas giants in various planetary systems. Good luck further developing this classification system!
Describe Neptunian gas giant

A Neptunian gas giant would be a type of gas giant planet similar in characteristics to the ice giants Neptune and Uranus in our own Solar System. These Neptunian gas giants are challenging to classify using traditional classification systems like the Sudarsky classes due to their unique features.

Neptunian gas giants are likely to have atmospheres rich in hydrogen, helium, and other gases, but with a significant amount of ices and compounds such as water, ammonia, and methane. These planets would have lower densities compared to gas giants dominated by hydrogen and helium, giving them their classification as Neptunian, after the ice giant Neptune.

These planets may exhibit distinctive features such as bright blue colors, strong atmospheric dynamics, possibly featuring high-speed winds and storm systems. Neptunian gas giants could also have complex cloud formations and atmospheric processes due to the presence of ices and organic compounds in their atmospheres.

Given the challenges in classifying these Neptunian gas giants within existing classification systems, further research and observations would be needed to better understand their unique properties and characteristics compared to other types of gas giants.
Describe Polaric gas giant

A Polaric gas giant would be a type of gas giant planet with characteristics similar to the gas giants in our own Solar System but distinguished by features specific to Jupiter's south pole. This classification would set them apart from the traditional Sudarsky classifications.

Polaric gas giants are theorized to have atmospheric conditions and dynamics mirroring those found at Jupiter's south pole. This could include unique cloud formations, storm systems, and atmospheric patterns that are distinct from other regions of the planet or other gas giants in general.

These gas giants may exhibit features such as polar vortices, cyclones, and other atmospheric phenomena specific to polar regions. They could also have different temperature gradients, atmospheric compositions, and cloud structures compared to gas giants without these polar characteristics.

Studying Polaric gas giants could provide valuable insights into the influence of polar regions on the overall climate and dynamics of gas giant planets. Further research and observations would be necessary to understand the specific characteristics and behaviors of these intriguing celestial bodies.
Describe Tholinitic gas giant

A Tholinitic gas giant would be a type of gas giant planet characterized by the presence of tholins in its atmosphere. Tholins are complex organic molecules that form through chemical reactions in the presence of ultraviolet light or cosmic rays, and they are commonly found in environments like the atmosphere of Saturn's moon Titan.

A Tholinitic gas giant would likely have a unique appearance and composition compared to other gas giants. The presence of tholins in its atmosphere could give the planet distinct colors and cloud formations. These tholins could contribute to the planet's overall chemistry and radiative properties, affecting its albedo and spectral characteristics.

Gas giants with tholinitic characteristics may have complex and dynamic atmospheres, with the presence of organic compounds influencing the planet's climate and atmospheric processes. The interaction between tholins and other gases in the atmosphere could lead to the formation of unique cloud structures and atmospheric phenomena.

Studying Tholinitic gas giants could offer valuable insights into the role of organic molecules in planetary atmospheres and how they impact the overall behavior and appearance of these celestial bodies. Further research and observations would be needed to understand the specific characteristics and implications of gas giants with tholinitic features.
Describe Venusian gas giant

A Venusian gas giant would be a type of gas giant planet that exhibits characteristics similar to those of the planet Venus in our own Solar System. Venus is a rocky planet with a thick atmosphere primarily composed of carbon dioxide and clouds of sulfuric acid, with extreme temperatures and atmospheric pressures.

A Venusian gas giant would likely have some unique features compared to traditional gas giants. This type of gas giant might have a predominantly carbon dioxide atmosphere, possibly with high concentrations of sulfur compounds or other elements present in Venus's atmosphere.

The extreme temperatures and pressures found on Venus could also influence the composition and structure of a Venusian gas giant. The thick clouds of sulfuric acid present in Venus's atmosphere could manifest in the form of unique cloud formations on a Venusian gas giant, affecting the planet's appearance and radiative properties.

Additionally, the high temperatures and pressures on a Venusian gas giant could lead to interesting atmospheric dynamics and processes, shaping the planet's climate and weather patterns in distinct ways compared to other gas giants in the universe.

Studying Venusian gas giants could provide valuable insights into the potential diversity of gas giant planets and the influence of different factors, such as extreme temperatures and atmospheric compositions, on their overall characteristics and behavior. Further research and observations would be needed to better understand the specific properties and implications of gas giants with Venusian-like features.
Describe Sulfurian gas giant

A Sulfurian gas giant would be a type of gas giant planet characterized by the presence of sulfur clouds in its atmosphere. Sulfur is a chemical element that can form compounds in various states at different temperatures and pressures, leading to unique atmospheric features and properties.

Gas giants with sulfurian characteristics may have clouds composed of sulfur compounds, such as sulfur dioxide or hydrogen sulfide, which could give the planet a distinctive color and appearance. These sulfur clouds could interact with other elements and compounds in the atmosphere, influencing the planet's overall chemistry and atmospheric processes.

The presence of sulfur clouds in the atmosphere of a Sulfurian gas giant could impact the planet's reflectivity, thermal properties, and spectral characteristics. Sulfur compounds are known to absorb and scatter light in different ways, potentially affecting the planet's albedo and radiative balance.

Studying Sulfurian gas giants could offer valuable insights into the role of sulfur in planetary atmospheres and its influence on the planet's climate, weather patterns, and overall appearance. Further research and observations would be necessary to understand the specific characteristics and implications of gas giants with sulfurian features and their contribution to the diversity of exoplanets in the universe.
Describe Rutilian gas giant

A Rutilian gas giant would be a type of gas giant planet characterized by the presence of titanium/vanadium oxides known as "rutiles" in its atmosphere. Rutiles are compounds that contain titanium and vanadium and can form in certain conditions within the atmospheres of planets.

Gas giants with Rutilian characteristics may exhibit unique optical properties due to the presence of rutiles in their atmosphere. These compounds can absorb and scatter light in specific ways, influencing the planet's color, albedo, and spectral signature. The interaction of rutiles with incoming radiation can impact the planet's thermal balance and radiative properties.

The presence of titanium/vanadium oxides "rutiles" in the atmosphere of a Rutilian gas giant could result in distinctive cloud formations and atmospheric phenomena. These compounds may play a role in shaping the planet's atmospheric dynamics and processes, influencing temperature gradients, cloud structures, and other meteorological features.

Studying Rutilian gas giants could provide valuable insights into the influence of titanium/vanadium oxides on planetary atmospheres and their potential impact on the planet's appearance and behavior. Further research and observations would be necessary to understand the specific characteristics and implications of gas giants with Rutilian features and their contribution to the diversity of gas giant planets in the universe.
Describe Siliconian gas giant

A Siliconian gas giant would be a type of gas giant planet characterized by the presence of silicones in its atmosphere. Silicones are a group of synthetic compounds that contain silicon, oxygen, carbon, and hydrogen, and they are known for their diverse chemical and physical properties.

Gas giants with Siliconian characteristics may have atmospheres rich in silicones, which could influence various aspects of the planet's atmospheric composition and behavior. The presence of silicones could contribute to the formation of unique cloud structures and atmospheric processes, potentially impacting the planet's climate and weather patterns.

The interaction of silicones with other gases and compounds in the atmosphere of a Siliconian gas giant could lead to intriguing optical properties and spectral signatures. Silicones are known to exhibit specific absorption and reflection characteristics, which could affect the planet's albedo and radiative properties.

Studying Siliconian gas giants could provide valuable insights into the role of silicones in planetary atmospheres and their potential influence on the planet's overall characteristics and behavior. Further research and observations would be necessary to better understand the specific properties and implications of gas giants with Siliconian features and their contribution to the diversity of gas giant planets in the universe.
Describe Corundian gas giant

A Corundian gas giant would be a type of gas giant planet characterized by the presence of corundum clouds in its atmosphere. Corundum is a crystalline form of aluminum oxide that can exhibit different colors depending on impurities present, with ruby and sapphire being well-known varieties of corundum.

Gas giants with Corundian characteristics may have cloud decks composed of corundum particles, which could give the planet a unique appearance and coloration. The presence of corundum clouds in the atmosphere could lead to the planet exhibiting hues of red, blue, or other colors associated with corundum.

The optical properties of corundum clouds could affect the planet's albedo, spectral signature, and overall radiative properties. The scattering and absorption of light by corundum particles could influence the planet's thermal balance and atmospheric dynamics, contributing to its climate and weather patterns.

Studying Corundian gas giants could offer valuable insights into the influence of corundum clouds on planetary atmospheres and their role in shaping the planet's appearance and behavior. Further research and observations would be needed to understand the specific characteristics and implications of gas giants with Corundian features and their contribution to the diversity of gas giant planets in the universe.
Describe Carbonytrogenic gas giant

A Carbonytrogenic gas giant would be a type of gas giant planet characterized by the presence of clouds composed of carbon monoxide and nitrogen in its atmosphere. Carbon monoxide (CO) and nitrogen (N2) are both common gases found in planetary atmospheres, and their combination could lead to unique atmospheric properties and behaviors.

Gas giants with Carbonytrogenic characteristics may exhibit cloud formations primarily consisting of carbon monoxide and nitrogen compounds, which could affect the planet's appearance and radiative properties. The interaction between these gases could influence the planet's albedo, spectral signature, and thermal balance.

The presence of carbon monoxide and nitrogen clouds in the atmosphere of a Carbonytrogenic gas giant could impact the planet's atmospheric dynamics, temperature gradients, and weather patterns. These compounds may play a role in shaping the planet's climate and atmospheric processes, contributing to its overall characteristics and behavior.

Studying Carbonytrogenic gas giants could provide insights into the role of carbon monoxide and nitrogen in planetary atmospheres and their influence on the planet's climate and atmospheric composition. Further research and observations would be necessary to understand the specific properties and implications of gas giants with Carbonytrogenic features and their contribution to the diversity of gas giant planets in the universe.
Describe Neonic gas giant

A Neonic gas giant would be a type of gas giant planet characterized by the presence of an icy composition with an ocean of liquid neon in its interior. Neon is a noble gas known for its inert and stable properties, and it typically exists as a gas under most conditions. However, under extreme pressure and temperature conditions, neon can exist in a liquid state.

Gas giants with Neonic characteristics would likely have a unique composition compared to traditional gas giants. The presence of an ocean of liquid neon within the planet could play a significant role in shaping its internal structure and dynamics. The interaction between the neon ocean and other elements present in the planet's atmosphere could influence its overall behavior and characteristics.

The icy composition of a Neonic gas giant, coupled with the presence of a liquid neon ocean, could impact the planet's thermal properties, atmospheric processes, and geological features. The neon ocean may affect the planet's internal heat distribution and potentially contribute to its magnetic field generation.

Studying Neonic gas giants could offer valuable insights into the diversity of planetary compositions and the influence of exotic elements such as liquid neon on planetary evolution and dynamics. Further research and observations would be necessary to understand the specific properties and implications of gas giants with Neonic features and their contribution to the variety of gas giant planets in the universe.
